title: Small Filters, Big Context
concept: CNNs often stack several small filters because the stack can cover a large region step by step, while also giving the network extra stages for learning useful patterns.

exercises.prompt: Recap: About how large a receptive field can three stacked stride-1 3×3 layers create?
exercises.explain: Right — stacking small layers keeps growing the effective visible patch.

cards.title: Why not just use one huge filter?
cards.body: If multiple small layers can eventually see a big region, why would engineers bother with them? Why not jump straight to a giant filter and be done?

cards.title: Big context in stages
cards.body: Several small filters can build a large receptive field step by step. Instead of one giant jump, the network gets multiple chances to combine simple patterns into richer ones.

cards.title: Stairs instead of one giant leap
cards.body: Climbing a staircase gets you to a high floor through many manageable steps. A giant leap tries to do everything at once. Stacked small filters are the staircase version of reaching a larger view.

cards.title: Three 3×3s vs. one 7×7
cards.body: Three stacked 3×3 layers can cover about the same 7×7 area that one big 7×7 layer would cover. But the stack can first notice edges, then corners, then larger shapes — building understanding in stages instead of one giant sweep.

cards.title: Small does not mean weak
cards.body: A small filter is not automatically less powerful. When many small layers work together, later neurons can still gather broad context — and often in a more flexible, layered way.

cards.title: One Big Jump vs. Three Smart Steps
cards.body: Both paths can reach a wide area, but they do it differently:

left.text:
One giant look
at a wide patch
all at once

right.text:
Small looks added together
to reach similar context
while building patterns in stages

exercises.prompt: Why might a CNN use several small filters instead of one huge filter?

options.text: Because the stack can still reach big context while learning patterns step by step
Because small filters prevent the model from learning anything
Because receptive fields shrink when layers stack
Because the color black is easier to compute than white

exercises.explain: Exactly — multiple small layers can grow context gradually and add more stages of feature building.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A single giant filter is the only way to give a CNN broad context.
exercises.explain: False — stacked small filters can also create a large receptive field.

exercises.sentence: Several small layers can build a big ___.
exercises.explain: Context — that's the whole point of growing the receptive field step by step.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best captures the small-filters idea?

options.text: Three small layers can cover a wide region while also creating intermediate feature-building steps
Small filters are useless unless they immediately see the whole image
The only purpose of filter size is making diagrams look different

exercises.explain: Right — small filters can still support a big final view, but they get there in stages.

exercises.prompt: A classmate says, 'If I need a wide view, I must use one huge filter.' What's the best reply?

options.text: Not necessarily — several small filters can grow a wide receptive field step by step and build intermediate patterns along the way
Yes, because receptive fields never grow when layers stack
Yes, because only the first layer matters in a CNN
Yes, because small filters can only read one pixel total

exercises.explain: Exactly. CNNs often prefer stacked small filters because they can still reach broad context without doing everything in one giant jump. Next, you'll see why the size of that context matters for different tasks.
